title: Projectile Motion
learningObjectives: Resolve projectile motion into independent horizontal and vertical components
Calculate range, maximum height and time of flight
Apply SUVAT equations to each component independently
introText: Projectile motion describes the curved path of an object launched into the air, subject only to gravity. The key insight is that horizontal and vertical motions are independent.

steps.heading: What is a Projectile?
steps.body:
A projectile is any object that is launched and then moves freely under gravity alone (ignoring air resistance).

Examples: a thrown ball, a bullet, a kicked football.

The path followed is called a trajectory. For a projectile, it is a parabola.
steps.deepDive: Air resistance makes real trajectories asymmetric – the object decelerates horizontally and the descent is steeper than the ascent.

steps.heading: Horizontal and Vertical Independence
steps.body:
Horizontal motion: constant velocity (no acceleration)
vx = v₀ cosθ
x = vx × t

Vertical motion: uniform acceleration due to gravity (g = 9.81 m/s² downward)
vy₀ = v₀ sinθ
vy = vy₀ − gt
y = vy₀t − ½gt²

The time of flight is determined by the vertical motion only.
steps.deepDive: This independence principle was discovered by Galileo. He rolled a ball off a table and showed the time to hit the ground was the same as a ball dropped vertically from the same height.

steps.heading: Projectile Simulator
steps.instructionText: Adjust speed, angle and launch height. Click Launch to animate the trajectory and see calculated range, max height and time of flight.

steps.questionText: A ball is launched horizontally at 10 m/s from a height of 20 m. How long does it take to hit the ground? (g = 10 m/s²)
steps.explanation: Vertical: 20 = ½ × 10 × t², t² = 4, t = 2 s. The horizontal velocity doesn't affect the fall time.

questions.questionText: A projectile is launched at 30° to the horizontal at 20 m/s. What is the initial vertical component of velocity?
